title: Why repeated advice slowly stops being useful
summary: Argue that the more often a piece of advice is repeated in a culture, the less weight it tends to carry — and what people should do about that.
topic: Why over-repeated advice loses its meaning
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing that some kinds of advice lose their meaning the more they are repeated. Give one specific example, address a counter-objection, and use the present perfect, a second conditional, modals, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: There is a particular kind of advice that almost everyone in Mexico City seems to give and almost no one seems to follow, which is…
If you stopped a hundred adults on the street and asked them whether the advice they hear most often has actually changed how they live, you would find that…
Although the advice itself may be correct, what I have come to think is that the act of repeating it so often quietly destroys…

hintVocab.meaningVi: đưa lời khuyên không được yêu cầu
độ đáng tin của một thông điệp
có thiện ý nhưng không hữu ích
rơi vào tai điếc, không ai nghe

sampleText:
There is a particular kind of advice that almost everyone in Mexico City seems to give and almost no one seems to follow: 'Save for emergencies, never depend on a single income, and always read the contract.' I have heard it from uncles at weddings, from radio hosts on the morning commute, and from school principals at graduation ceremonies, often word for word. The advice is not wrong. In fact, it is so obviously correct that almost no adult in the country would publicly disagree with it. What I have come to suspect, however, is that the very frequency with which it is repeated has emptied it of force. We have heard it so many times that we have stopped actually hearing it, the way a person who lives next to a busy street eventually stops noticing the traffic.

If you stopped a hundred adults on the street and asked them whether this advice has actually changed how they live, you would find an uncomfortable gap. Many would tell you they nod politely whenever an older relative repeats it at dinner, and that they intend to follow it 'soon'. The same gap shows up with other heavily-repeated messages — about smoking, exercise, screen time, and saving for retirement. Although the message remains correct, the act of repeating it on every possible occasion teaches the listener to switch off the moment it begins. Advertising researchers have long known that a message can become invisible from overexposure, and there is no reason this would not also apply to advice given in living rooms and classrooms. A teacher who reminds a class to study harder every single morning soon trains them to stop hearing the reminder; the words become background, like a ringtone the brain has learned to ignore.

However, there is a fair counter-objection worth taking seriously. Some would argue that important advice should be repeated precisely because it is important, and that the alternative — silence — would be worse. I do not think this is quite right. The choice is not between empty repetition and silence; it is between mechanical repetition and the kind of advice that is offered carefully, occasionally, and in a specific situation that calls for it. When a colleague in Mexico City told me, after watching me sign a contract without reading the second page, that I should always read the small print, the advice landed differently from the same words shouted at me by a radio host. It came at the right moment, from someone who knew my situation, and it cost the speaker something to say. Advice given that way is hard to forget. Advice that has been worn smooth by ten thousand mouths usually goes in one ear and out the other.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the very frequency with which it is repeated has emptied it of force

sampleAnnotations.phrase: We have heard it so many times that we have stopped actually hearing it

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for an accumulated effect
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'We have heard it so many times that we have stopped actually hearing it' dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt một sự lặp lại tích lũy từ quá khứ đến hiện tại và một hệ quả vẫn còn đúng. Cấu trúc 'so + adverb + that' nối nguyên nhân với kết quả.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'We have heard it so many times that we have stopped actually hearing it' uses the present perfect to express an accumulation from the past to the present and a consequence still in effect. The 'so + adverb + that' structure links cause and result.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for a hypothetical survey
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you stopped a hundred adults on the street and asked them..., you would find an uncomfortable gap' là điều kiện loại 2 — quá khứ đơn ở mệnh đề if, 'would + nguyên thể' ở mệnh đề chính — dùng cho một tình huống giả định không thực sự diễn ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you stopped a hundred adults on the street and asked them..., you would find an uncomfortable gap' is the second conditional — past simple in the if-clause, 'would + base verb' in the main — used for a hypothetical situation that is not actually happening.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một loại lời khuyên đặc biệt mà gần như ai ở Mexico City cũng đưa ra và gần như không ai làm theo: 'Tiết kiệm cho lúc khẩn cấp, đừng bao giờ phụ thuộc vào một nguồn thu nhập duy nhất, và luôn đọc hợp đồng.' Tôi đã nghe nó từ các chú bác ở đám cưới, từ những người dẫn chương trình phát thanh trên đường đi làm buổi sáng, và từ hiệu trưởng tại lễ tốt nghiệp, thường là từng chữ một. Lời khuyên không sai. Thực ra, nó đúng đến mức gần như không một người lớn nào trong nước sẽ công khai phản đối nó. Tuy nhiên, điều tôi đã dần nghi ngờ là chính tần suất mà nó được lặp lại đã làm nó mất đi sức mạnh. Chúng ta đã nghe nó quá nhiều lần đến mức chúng ta đã thực sự ngừng nghe nó, giống như một người sống cạnh một con phố đông đúc cuối cùng ngừng nhận ra tiếng xe cộ.

Nếu bạn dừng một trăm người lớn trên đường và hỏi họ liệu lời khuyên này có thực sự thay đổi cách họ sống hay không, bạn sẽ tìm thấy một khoảng trống khó chịu. Nhiều người sẽ nói với bạn rằng họ gật đầu lịch sự bất cứ khi nào một người họ hàng lớn tuổi lặp lại nó vào bữa tối, và rằng họ định làm theo 'sớm thôi'. Cùng một khoảng trống xuất hiện với các thông điệp được lặp lại nhiều khác — về hút thuốc, tập thể dục, thời gian dùng màn hình, và tiết kiệm cho hưu trí. Mặc dù thông điệp vẫn đúng, hành động lặp lại nó vào mọi dịp có thể dạy người nghe tắt chú ý ngay khi nó bắt đầu. Các nhà nghiên cứu quảng cáo từ lâu đã biết rằng một thông điệp có thể trở nên vô hình do tiếp xúc quá nhiều, và không có lý do gì điều này không cũng áp dụng cho lời khuyên được đưa ra trong phòng khách và lớp học. Một giáo viên nhắc lớp học chăm chỉ hơn mỗi sáng sớm muộn sẽ rèn họ ngừng nghe lời nhắc; các từ trở thành nền, giống như một nhạc chuông mà não đã học cách lờ đi.

Tuy nhiên, có một phản đối công bằng đáng được xem xét nghiêm túc. Một số người sẽ cho rằng lời khuyên quan trọng nên được lặp lại chính vì nó quan trọng, và rằng lựa chọn thay thế — sự im lặng — sẽ tệ hơn. Tôi không nghĩ điều này hoàn toàn đúng. Lựa chọn không phải giữa lặp lại trống rỗng và im lặng; nó là giữa sự lặp lại máy móc và loại lời khuyên được đưa ra cẩn thận, thỉnh thoảng, và trong một tình huống cụ thể đòi hỏi nó. Khi một đồng nghiệp ở Mexico City nói với tôi, sau khi nhìn tôi ký một hợp đồng mà không đọc trang thứ hai, rằng tôi nên luôn đọc các điều khoản nhỏ, lời khuyên đó tác động khác với cùng những từ ấy được người dẫn chương trình phát thanh hét vào tai tôi. Nó đến đúng lúc, từ một người biết hoàn cảnh của tôi, và việc nói ra đã khiến người nói phải đánh đổi điều gì đó. Lời khuyên được đưa ra theo cách đó khó quên. Lời khuyên đã bị mòn nhẵn bởi mười nghìn cái miệng thường vào tai này ra tai kia.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly for accumulated experience?
options: I have heared it from uncles at weddings and from radio hosts on the morning commute.
I am hearing it from uncles at weddings and from radio hosts on the morning commute.
I have hear it from uncles at weddings and from radio hosts on the morning commute.
I have heard it from uncles at weddings and from radio hosts on the morning commute.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have + past participle'. The past participle of 'hear' is the irregular form 'heard'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If you stopped a hundred adults and asked them, you would find an uncomfortable gap.
If you stop a hundred adults and ask them, you would find an uncomfortable gap.
If you stopped a hundred adults and asked them, you will find an uncomfortable gap.
If you would stop a hundred adults and ask them, you would find an uncomfortable gap.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'so + adverb + that' to link cause and result?
options: We have heard it so much times that we have stopped actually hearing it.
We have heard it so many times that we have stopped actually hearing it.
We have heard it so many times so that we have stopped actually hearing it.
We have heard it that many times so we have stopped actually hearing it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'So + many + countable noun + that + result' is the standard pattern; 'many times' (countable), not 'much times'.

options: Although the message remains correct, but the act of repeating it teaches the listener to switch off.
Although, the message remains correct the act of repeating it teaches the listener to switch off.
Although the message remains correct, the act of repeating it teaches the listener to switch off.
The message remains correct although however the act of repeating it teaches the listener to switch off.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling with 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'however' to introduce a contrasting paragraph?
options: However there is a fair counter-objection worth taking seriously.
However; there is a fair counter-objection worth taking seriously.
However, there is a fair counter-objection worth to take seriously.
However, there is a fair counter-objection worth taking seriously.
mcQuestions.explanation: Sentence-initial 'however' takes a comma. A semicolon is wrong, and 'worth' is followed by '-ing', not 'to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'modal + base verb' correctly?
options: Important advice should be repeated precisely because it is important.
Important advice should to be repeated precisely because it is important.
Important advice should being repeated precisely because it is important.
Important advice should be repeat precisely because it is important.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal passive: 'should be + past participle'. 'Should' + bare infinitive 'be'; participle 'repeated'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'no reason + clause' correctly?
options: There is no reason this would not also apply for advice given in living rooms.
There is no reason this would not also apply to advice given in living rooms.
There is no reason for which this would not also apply at advice given in living rooms.
There is no a reason this would not also apply to advice given in living rooms.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'There is no reason + clause' is standard; 'apply to' is the correct collocation; 'no reason' takes no article.

options: There is a fair counter-objection worth take seriously.
There is a fair counter-objection worth to take seriously.
There is a fair counter-objection worth taking seriously.
There is a fair counter-objection worthy of take seriously.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth + -ing form' is the standard structure. 'Worth to' is wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'the choice is not between X and Y; it is between X and Z' correctly?
options: The choice is not between empty repetition and silence; it is between mechanical repetition and careful advice between.
The choice not is between empty repetition and silence; it is between mechanical repetition and careful advice.
The choice is not between empty repetition or silence; it is between mechanical repetition or careful advice.
The choice is not between empty repetition and silence; it is between mechanical repetition and careful advice.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Between X and Y' is the fixed pattern (not 'between X or Y'); the parallel structure must use 'and' both times.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'go in one ear and out the other'?
options: Advice that has been worn smooth by ten thousand mouths usually goes in one ear and out the other.
Advice that has been worn smooth by ten thousand mouths usually goes on one ear and out the other.
Advice that has been worn smooth by ten thousand mouths usually goes in one ear and out other.
Advice that has been worn smooth by ten thousand mouths usually go in one ear and out the other.
mcQuestions.explanation: The fixed idiom is 'go in one ear and out the other'. Article 'the' is required before 'other'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Goes in one ear and out the other'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'precisely because' correctly to give an emphatic reason?
options: Important advice should be repeated precise because it is important.
Important advice should be repeated precisely because it is important.
Important advice should be repeated because precisely it is important.
Important advice should be repeated precisely for it is important.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Precisely because + clause' is the standard emphatic form. 'Precisely' is the adverb form; 'because' (not 'for') introduces the clause.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If advice loses power when repeated, but new advice is rarely listened to either, how would you actually transmit important information across a generation? Who is responsible — the speaker, the listener, or the institutions in between?
